The[ current Pypi version](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/gps3/0.30.3) lags the Github in trivial typos fixes and the value change and move of the `sleep(.3)` during the iteration of the socket.

The previous location would hammer the socket with 'it is here, yet?' between a sleep designed to keep it from hammering the entire time.

The current locations will sleep 0.3 - 0.1seconds between empty sockets.  Specific time is trivial. There is less granularity in cpu load for this location than the old, i.e., ranging 0.1-1.0 has less effect on load range than 0.1-1.0 did for the old, but offers faster response for this less cpu load.

The Pypi package has not been update with these incremental, or typographical fixes to consolidate them and forestall the 'version inflation'  from a trail of build numbers, as Pypi won't update with incrementing.  They mostly effect the examples.

That being said, they should be synced, or harnessed more closely.

This is definitely not an issue, and the more I think about it, the less I see a clean solution to this problem. The thing is that if you have a retry rules which states "retry if the result is None for 10 times", then your function _will_ raise `RetryError`, no matter what.

So it means depending on the retrying condition, the error raised might be different. I don't think it's a good design per say, and I think fixing the function using retrying to handle that condition correctly is the good choice (like you did).

However, we could help by having a `reraise()` method so you could wrote:

```python
try:
    my_retried_fn()
except tenacity.RetryError as retry_err:
    retry_err.reraise()
```

Though if the `RetryError` has no exception (because e.g. it retried 10 times because the result was None) we would _anyway_ raise the `RetryError`.

Finally I have found the error. There is a bug in the dc power flow function of the Pypower Version 4.1.2. In the "dcpf.py" function in the statement:

Va[pvpq] = spsolve(B[pvpq.T, pvpq], Pbus[pvpq] - B[pvpq.T, ref] * Va0[ref])

The second argument to the spsolve function is a row vector instead of the column vector. The shape[0] command in the spsolve thus produces matrix dimension error. Just transposing it resolves the error.

Curio is not compatible with asyncio so it's unlikely that any of those functions would run on the kernel.   Interoperability with asyncio is an open issue that would require investigation.

Here's another starvation case. It's not realistic, but the effect is still a bit surprising. We have two tasks that talk to each other (here they happen to be identical, but you can imagine them implementing some sort of RPC pattern or something), and they actually *block* on each iteration -- i.e., are removed entirely from the ready queue. So you'd think they couldn't starve the kernel! Yet together they do, because they take turns putting each other back onto the ready queue, so that it never empties out:

```python
import curio

async def watchdog():
    i = 0
    while True:
        print("Event loop is live after {} seconds".format(i))
        i += 1
        await curio.sleep(1)

async def ping(inq, outq):
    while True:
        obj = await inq.get()
        # ... do some processing ...
        await outq.put(obj)

async def pong(inq, outq):
    while True:
        obj = await inq.get()
        # ... do some processing ...
        await outq.put(obj)

async def main():
    q1 = curio.Queue()
    q2 = curio.Queue()
    await q1.put("something")
    await curio.spawn(watchdog())
    await curio.spawn(ping(q1, q2))
    await curio.spawn(pong(q2, q1))

curio.run(main())
```

It prints "Event loop is live" once, and then goes silent. If we add a call to `curio.sleep(0)` inside of either of the `ping` / `pong` tasks (doesn't matter which one) then it starts printing the message once a second, as intended.

I guess this could be an argument for splitting the ready queue into two pieces: a "current" queue and a "pending" queue. The idea would be that when a task becomes runnable it goes onto the pending queue, never the current queue. The current queue gets processed until its empty, and then gets refilled by checking for I/O + timeouts + the pending queue. So that would enforce the invariant that any single task can't get scheduled twice without an I/O check in between. I don't know if it would affect any realistic cases, but it would certainly make it easier to prove that these kinds of weird cases aren't lurking :-)

(I discovered this while I was trying to figure out how to reliably print a diagnostic if some task blocks the event loop for too long. Specifically I was trying to figure out whether I should measure the time taken in an individual task execution (advantage: if a task does starve the kernel, we know who to blame), or the time between I/O checks (advantage: can catch multi-task pathologies like this).)

Instead of calling the state transition from the  thread, I set a boolean variable in the thread, and that is checked in the main loop. The "when" statement is wrong, it should be
```
 when "../physical-object='true'";
```
All string literal values have to be quoted because otherwise in this example the XPath processor tries to compare the values of two nodes (XML elements) namely `../physical-object` and `true`. Since the latter doesn't exist, the expression is always false.

> Notice how 'sfs__k_features': 3. In fact I can set that SFS k_features parameter to any value (say 6) because what counts is the 'sfs__k_features' : list(range(1, X.shape[1])), as these are the values of k_features that will be used by SFS in the grid search. SFS select the best sub-set of features of a given size, GridSearchCV selects the best sub-set size (in this case 3 features). Is this correct?

Yes! But prior to that, i.e., in your old version, you encountered this V-related dimensionality issue because you pre-computed the variance based on 4 features, then you used 3 features to feed it to the algo. Now, it doesn't matter anymore how you seed the k_features since you modify them during the grid search anyway; the `k_features = 4` becomes just a "placeholder".

> So here are you saying that for each of the SFS's 540 fits the last knn will be executed for 4 different neighbour sizes (4x540 = 2160)? Doesn't the last knn simply used the best neighbour size from 'sfs__estimator__n_neighbors' : list(k_range), list? In't it simpy repeating the same calculations?

Yeah, that sounds correct. So basically, it works like this now:

1. initialize pipepeline with: MinMaxScaler(), SFS with its own KNN, a different KNN classifier

2. 
    1. put training fold through standard scaler
    2. select k_features via SFS using its knn as "evaluator" with `n_neighbors`
    3. feed best `k_features` to the separate KNN classifier with `n_neighbors`
3. Go back to step 2

Or maybe let's say we have a LogisticRegression classifier in the SFS to make thinks more clear 

```python
sfs1 = SFS(estimator=LogisticRegression(),
           k_features=4,
           forward=True,
           floating=False,
           scoring='accuracy',
           print_progress=False,
           cv=5)
           # !?!? n_jobs=-1)

pipe = Pipeline([
                 ('standardize', preprocessing.MinMaxScaler()),
                 ('sfs', sfs1),
                 ('knn', knn)])

```

1. initialize pipepeline with: MinMaxScaler(), SFS withLogisticRegression

2. 
    1. put training fold through standard scaler
    2. select k_features via SFS using LogisticRegression with an e.g., specific regularization strength
    3. feed best `k_features` to the KNN classifier with `n_neighbors`
3. Go back to step 2

Now, you can use the same classifier instance for both selection and classification, e.g., 


```python

lr = LogisticRegression()

sfs1 = SFS(estimator=lr,
           clone=False,
           k_features=4,
           forward=True,
           floating=False,
           scoring='accuracy',
           print_progress=False,
           cv=5)
           # !?!? n_jobs=-1)

pipe = Pipeline([
                 ('standardize', preprocessing.MinMaxScaler()),
                 ('sfs', sfs1),
                 ('clf', lr)])

```
However, in your case, we have the problem then that the V dimensions from SFS don't match the classifier's in the pipeline and vice versa

> Invalid cyphertext, shorter than crypto_secretstream_xchacha20poly1305_ABYTES
well it means that the message is shorter than the minimum message length, it's a corrupted message, one that hasn't even a poly1305 tag. you know the cyphertext is "message+tag" where the length of the tag is crypto_secretstream_xchacha20poly1305_ABYTES, and if the message does not even have a tag then the message is invalid. maybe "truncated ciphertext" is a better message.

About MQTT, personally I do not use Ethernet yet - we have 42 Arduinos working and had no cash for Ethernet Shields for them left as we preferred to get more sensors - so we communicate mostly over USB/Serial - it would be interesting to have, but not a priority for me - next big extension of lab setup would be in late 2015 or 2016.

About callback - being able to tell which pins to report is indeed nice option - so you plan something like:

    registerCallback({"A0", "13"}, closure)

would be the way to go, where on callback it would be called with

    closure(A0=value, 13=value)

But - what if we find change on 13 only - should we then poll the other pins for their values? Or - if we do some sensor which have 3 outputs and we will get 3 callbacks very close to each other - do we somehow filter those two when not all dimensions were updated yet?

While being able to specify more than one pin/device would be nice, it might be quite complicated to get right in generic way. It might need to provide a configurable behaviour for different scenarios.

I believe, that for now, simple interface will be enough, and this could be an advanced feature in next version - for now, just being able to assign callback to single pin, would be enough, and let the user of library deal with logic. Interesting use cases might come up in field use of this.

I hotfixed it like this for ``python 3.6.3`` and ``pypac 0.3.1``, and it seems to work for ``python2`` and ``python3``:

    def get_proxies(self, url):
        """
        Get the proxies that are applicable to a given URL, according to the PAC file.

        :param str url: The URL for which to find appropriate proxies.
        :return: All the proxies that apply to the given URL.
            Can be empty, which means to abort the request.
        :rtype: list[str]
        """
        parsed_url = urlparse(url)
        host = str(parsed_url.netloc).split(':')[0]
        value_from_js_func = self.pac.find_proxy_for_url(url, host)
        if value_from_js_func in self._cache:
            return self._cache[value_from_js_func]

        config_values = parse_pac_value(self.pac.find_proxy_for_url(url, host), self.socks_scheme)
        if self._proxy_auth:
            config_values = [add_proxy_auth(value, self._proxy_auth) for value in config_values]

        self._cache[value_from_js_func] = config_values

        return config_values

Mainly because that would make consuming the library too messy, as it would require updating downstream build systems every minor release (due to the version being part of the identifier). People can always request a minimum (or maximum) version with pkg-config, like `dlib-1 >= 18.15`, but this doesn't guarantee parallel installability. Given the current state of the library, I assume most interfaces to be pretty solid, not? Hence a _major_ versioning scheme should be preferred. Or said differently, version as much as needed (backwards incompatible API changes) but as little as possible (i.e., dlib-18.17 and dlib-18.18 is too much and will again be too much of a hassle to be usable).

A major problem is the fact that elements refer to the nodes not by index, but by a tag that associated with each node. >in _event_loop_wrappers.py, maybe use consistent naming (curio_sendall and trio_send_all)

The naming inconsistencies here reflect the inconsistencies between naming in the libraries. For example, curio has `socket.sendall` where trio has `socket.send_all`. This way it just keeps track of the underlying library's naming.

> you can just remove the else in session.BaseSession._connect and unindent the following return statement

Going to leave that as is just for explicit-ness' sake.

> session.BaseSession.timeout_manager looks crazy, but I don't know the details well enough to fix it (think it might be bugged too).

Confirmed it's quite crazy looking. I've yet to have an issue with it, but would like to hear where your concern lies. 

The crazyness here stemms from a few things:

Firstly, in curio  `TaskGroup` instanciation using a context manager requires an async context manager, so `async with`. However trio requires a regular syncronous `with`. The lack of `aenter` / `aexit` triggers the attribute error.

Secondly, curio's `TaskGroup.spawn()` requires an `await` where trio's `nursery.spawn()` requires you do not `await`.

Thirdly, grabbing the result of a  task in curio only requires you access the `.result` attribute, where as trio requires you access `.result.unwrap()`

These three differences compound and make for some odd looking code here. To be honest, it could probably be abstracted further, but I'm not sure how much of a benefit there would be to burying this deeper. It's certainly something to be looked in to though.

Hey @mehaase, thanks for bringing this up!

What were your experiences with using a recurring job every day to check for the 1st of the month for this use case?

One thing I'd be worried about with adding monthly and yearly intervals is that it becomes easier for folks to use schedule in a way that's error prone. For example:

* Long intervals between job runs make it easier for things to go wrong. For example a re-deploy leads to a process restart that resets the timer.
* For long intervals we'd probably need support for job persistency in order to make sure we don't run jobs more often than necessary or miss job runs (This relates to https://github.com/dbader/schedule/issues/17)

Of course, one could always use something like `every(30).days.do(...)` to schedule longer intervals. But again I'd be worried that we're encouraging a pattern that is error prone.  For mission-critical stuff that absolutely must run once a year it's probably best to just use cron.

So maybe the pattern we want to encourage everyone to use is what you're doing currently, i.e run a "check-in" job every day and see if we need to trigger a particular task. That way things will continue to work across process restarts etc. Thoughts?

because this folder is used as temporary to upload `docker-compose.yaml` on remote server. By default Fabricio uses `/tmp` for this purpose.

Euhm, I am getting confused. If you run "unoconv --listener", the aim is to run one instance of soffice.bin to do all subsequent conversions for you.

If you don't run "unoconv --listener", it will start an soffice.bin listener on every invocation, and then tears it down after use. So doing "unoconv --listener --format=pdf file.odt" makes not sense to me. Just do "unoconv --format=pdf file.odt" instead, or even "unoconv file.odt" as PDF is the default output format.

I'm not sure that a new SourceGenerator instance would buy you anything when you get to multi-line comments, though.  In fact, I'd probably consider ditching the context manager and just recording the current length of the result list at the start of the comment method, and then post-processing the sublist that starts after that (deleting it off the end of the result list if that makes it easier) to fix the indentation and add the comment character to all the lines in the sublist.

Unfortunately, the pretty printer in astor.source_repr will wreak havoc with your nicely commented lines if they are too long.  For example, I suspect that if you make the expression in your current test case long enough, it will fail miserably.

You could consider modifying the pretty printer, but that's really ugly code, and I dare the author to correct me :-).   What I would do instead is this:  when you are post-processing the comment lines to fix the indentation and add comment characters, before you do any of that you can run the result sublist representing the commented code or text through astor.source_repr.split_lines with a modified maxline parameter.  Then you can strip the indentation and add the comment character to the start of every line.  The code would look something like this:

     # add split_lines and flatten to the import at the top
    from .source_repr import pretty_source, split_lines, flatten

    def visit_Comment(self, node):
        prefix = '# '
        end_result = len(self.result)
        # Allowable line length for wrapping.  Allow extra for the indentation we 
        # will strip, but reduce based on the prefix we will add.
        max_line = 79 + len(self.indent_with) * self.indentation - len(prefix)
        --- add your text or visit your nodes here ---
        comment = source_repr.split_lines(result[end_result:], max_line)
        --- loop through comment, stripping indentation and adding prefix ---
        result[end_result:] = flatten(comment)

import asyncio
import aiorpcx
import ssl
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger('client')

class CashSession(aiorpcx.ClientSession):
    def header_notification(self, header):
        logger.info(f'notified of new header: {header}')

    def notification_handler(self, method):
        # We subscribe so might be unlucky enough to get a notification...
        if method == 'blockchain.headers.subscribe':
            return self.header_notification
        return None


async def main():
    sslc = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLS)
    event = asyncio.Event()
    async with CashSession('bch.electrumx.cash', 50002, ssl=sslc) as session:
        logger.info('connected')
        result = await session.send_request('server.version', ['test', '1.4'])
        logger.info(f'server.version: {result}')
        result = await session.send_request('blockchain.headers.subscribe')
        logger.info(f'subscription response: {result}')
        await event.wait()


logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main())

> First, note that your code can be rewritten as :
> ```
> await (stream.merge(*getters) | pipe.map(process))
> ```

Oooh, that's nicer.  🙂  Hadn't quite figured out where pipes come in to play...

So, a custom operator sounds like what I might need.  A (hopefully) less contrived example:

```python
class Transport:

    async def start(self):
        # Start queuing things up to be collected from receive().

    async def stop(self):
        # Request to finish up -- retrieve the last batch of messages etc.

    async def receive(self):
        # Implementation varies by subclass, but will always yield messages.
        while True:
            yield Message(...)
```

```python
class Host:

    self.transports = [Transport(...)]

    async def run(self):
        getters = (transport.receive() for transport in self.transports)
        await (aiostream.stream.merge(*getters) | aiostream.pipe.map(self.process))

    async def process(self, msg):
        if ... == "add-transport":  # e.g. from command processing
            transport = Transport(...)
            self.transports.append(transport)
            await transport.start()
            # We've added a new transport, so need to start receiving from it.
        elif ... == "remove-transport":
            transport = ...
            await transport.stop()
            self.transports.remove(transport)
            # Similarly, we need to stop receiving messages from this transport.
```

In reality, the processing is slightly more split up than this (handled by one or more instances of a separate class, managed similarly to transports), but the idea is the same -- the down-the-line processing may change the set of currently-active transports.

`Host.run` is effectively the entry point here (e.g. `loop.run_until_complete(host.run())`), and will run for the lifetime of the process, so I'm currently looking to tap in to the list of streams being merged.

I would expect the remove case to be resolved by making `receive()` return once stopped (which it probably ought to), which reduces the problem to just introducing new streams.  I think recreating the stream is too heavy for this -- it would mean stopping all the transports, clearing the queue (a few messages might still turn up after a stop request), and then restarting them again.
To be clear, this is not a spiceypy specific issue, but an environment issue (albeit likely a common one)

msgpack_numpy effectively serializes a dict containing the shape, contents (as raw bytes), and data type of a numpy object. For non-Python msgpack implementations, one would need to write a decoder that deserializes each of those pieces of information and uses them to instantiate and set a suitable data structure for whatever language was used for that implementation (e.g., an [Eigen](http://eigen.tuxfamily.org/) array for C++). I haven't investigated whether there are existing C++ msgpack decoders that can unpack encoded Python dicts, though.

I'm sorry for the code modifications that were introduced by the last commits!
Them were necessary in order to produce a more readable and stable code and to reduce the amount of information that have to be propagated by the recursive calls.

At the current state the generated swagger specs (due to the ``dereference_served_schema`` parameter) are self-contained.
Be self-contained means that the ``$ref`` tags have to target the current file.
Since we can still use the ``$ref``s the code is fetching all the *external* references and putting them on the ``/definition/`` part of the specs. 

An additional test with the bravado client is ensuring that the generated swagger spec are compliant.

Brandon, I'm out of my depths here, but as someone who uses both openLDAP and Active Directory, it looks like you may be using Active Directory. In my past experience, connecting to A.D. from *nix requires spelling the field, 'sAMAccountName'. I hope this doesn't confuse the issue at all (I've moved on to using this wonderful package Dave has made), but here's a snippet of code I wrote a while back using the old python-ldap package, which might help:

    """
    This code provide an example of how to connect to LDAP (specifically, Active Directory)
    using Python 3.

    Requires python-ldap3, available via the following command:
    pip install git+https://github.com/rbarrois/python-ldap.git@py3
    """

    import ldap

    LDAP_URI = 'ldap://ldap.domain.com'
    LDAP_DN = 'dc=subdomain,dc=domain,dc=com'
    LDAP_USERNAME = 'ldapquery_django@domain.com'
    LDAP_PASSWORD = ''
    USER_NAME = 'apache'
    USER_IN_GROUP = 'CN=SQLAdmins,OU=MS SQL,OU=Service Accounts,DC=subdomain,DC=domain,DC=com'
    USER_NOT_IN_GROUP = 'CN=Some Fake Security Group,OU=Administrative Departments,DC=subdomain,DC=domain,DC=com'

    try:
        # Connect to LDAP / Active Directory
        ldap_con = ldap.initialize(LDAP_URI)
        ldap_con.protocol_version = 3
        ldap_con.set_option(ldap.OPT_REFERRALS, 0)
        ldap_con.simple_bind_s(LDAP_USERNAME, LDAP_PASSWORD)

        # sAMAAccountName is Active Directory's beautiful way to say, 'username'
        user_filter='(&(objectCategory=person)(objectClass=user)(sAMAccountName=' + USER_NAME + '))'
        attrs = ['memberOf']

        # Perform the search.
        ldap_user = ldap_con.search_s(LDAP_DN, ldap.SCOPE_SUBTREE, user_filter, attrs)

        # Active Directory returns a list of byte literals. Convert them to strings in a more sensibly named list.
        ldap_groups = []
        for value in ldap_user[0][1]['memberOf']:
            ldap_groups.append(value.decode('utf-8'))

        # Free up memory
        ldap_user = None

        # Print the LDAP groups the user above is a member of, one per line.
        for value in ldap_groups:
            print(value)

Hi @sobolevn, thanks for your feedback!

What kind of secret settings? databases or AWS credentials for example?
You can do it with two ways, with environment variables or config files placed on the host.

With environment, you can activate `OVERRIDE_BY_ENV` [special setting](http://simple-settings.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#special-settings), for instance:

```python
# settings.py
SIMPLE_SETTINGS = {'OVERRIDE_BY_ENV': True}
MY_SECRET = ''
```

and, do something like this:
```
$ export MY_SECRET="some really secret"
$ python my_app.py --simple-settings=settings
```
The simple-settings override the value of `MY_SECRET` with value of environment variable

With config files, you can create a simple KEY=VALUE file (or json or yaml) with your secrets and use this file in the simple-settings [load pipeline](http://simple-settings.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#load-multiple-settings-modules), for instance:

```
# secrets.cfg
MY_SECRET=some really secret
```
```
$ python my_app.py --settings-settings=settings,secrets.cfg
```

let me know if this works for you.

RoCE has been supported since [this PR](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/pull/9432). But I am not familiar with it since we don't use it. In our setup, we use Infiniband RDMA directly, then there is another ethernet adapter for TCP/IP. You did not say whether  pure tensorflow + RoCE worked for you or not. My suggestion is to go back to tensorflow, and turn on the RDMA option and see if it works for you. If it works as you expect, then come back to TensorflowOnSpark, continue the debug. 

We do not use any special version of Spark since we have ethernet connection. If SparkRDMA works for you, go for it.

For questions regarding RoCE and tensorflow, you can ask them on tensorflow github repo. You may find more experienced users there.

@macisamuele @sjaensch I think considering the swagger spec validator passes, we cannot force users to specify a type, even if it makes more sense semantically. The situation that @bobh66 is encountering is exactly the situation that we are hitting. Users are pre-validating the specs with swagger spec validator and then requests are failing at runtime. I have fixed some users by having them specify a type. But this doesn't even make sense for some cases such as [vendor extensions](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#patterned-objects-9), where the user might want to do their own interpretation of the object and a type would be semantically misleading. And it is certainly surprising for this to fail at runtime.

I think we'll eventually go the route of no validation. But I think for now, this approach is an improvement over the current behavior, which is broken for some users. At least in this case, it is somewhat less surprising.

The fix for #35 makes it so 'type' is required. The rationale being, definitions split up across multiple files are a little more involved to go track down and check for compliance. This is no longer just a matter of checking `#/definitions` in the root spec.

Initial thoughts: Preface- This is long. *Please don't hate me!* I think the PR is great, but diving deeper into it with some integrated test cases based on usage, it seems bleach was broken in other unreported ways and this introduces some larger/undesirable regressions on a "usage" scale. I was trying to think about usage in terms of end-users and use-cases, not just unit tests. Going through all this, I think bleach performed a few functions in a 'not great' technical manner, but few people cared because the end-result was fine. It seems like this PR takes a technically correct and elegant approach to solving some issues, but risks breaking current usage patterns because the inelegant approach 'just worked'. 1- The clarification on HTML attributes looks good. It's not necessary due to the fixes PR, but a good idea (our issue was that we put "attribute-safe" data into bleach, but it was decoded then "cleaned" into an unsafe format.) 2- I'm worried by the loss of idempotent transforms. It doesn't affect us, but this has the potential to really break stuff. I know it may not be avoidable. IMHO this would need to be a 2.1 not 2.0.1 release with a big warning. 3- The new encoding behavior will break installs where html entities are entered, which includes many wyswig markdown editors. consider this high-level use-case that is largely oblivious to what is going on: comment = Comment(user_id) comment.content = bleach.clean('•') session.save(comment) # will store to utf-8 the old behavior would decode the `•` to the unicode point `u'\u2022'`; then the database would store it as utf-8 behind the scenes. bleach.clean('•').encode('utf-8') '\xe2\x80\xa2' when this gets rendered out, a bullet still renders on the HTML page. it's no longer a `•` but the unicode character... however viewers will still see a bulletpoint. under the new transformations, `•` will be appear where a bulletpoint is expected (as `u'•'` is stored in the database) This is a GIANT change and likely to adversely affect many. 4- A tiny breaking change is how bleach handles the "special" `cgi.escape` entities if entered in the numeric form. previously these were transcoded from numeric to text. input = """&<>""" 1.4.3 and 2.0.0: bleach.clean("""&<>""") u'&<>' pr#293 u'&<>' I doubt anyone relied on this or realized it, but I did want to mention it. 5- The clean and linkify commands on query strings don't create the same output, still break links, and don't necessarily work as expected: plaintext_url = 'http://example.com?active=true¤t=true' bleach.clean(plaintext_url) u'http://example.com?active=true&current=true' html = 'http://example.com?active=true¤t=true' bleach.clean(html) u'http://example.com?active=true&current=true' bleach.linkify(plaintext_url) u'http://example.com?active=true\xa4t=true' So a few things on the above. `clean` if we're just dealing with a plaintext_url, the encoding of the `&` upgrades this into an "html display of a url". this is a bit of a conceptual shift and may break implementations that don't expect plaintext url occurrences to be upgrade to html. For example, we run bleach before regexing input for urls (we track the domains/kwargs to identify spammers). We'll need to parse the output as html first now. if we're dealing with html input, this breaks the url in the href when there are more than one param(!). this appears to be previously broken back to at least 1.4.3 , so it's not a new problem - but it is a problem (I'll create a new issue for this) expected outputs would need to preserve the URL in attributes: u'http://example.com?active=true¤t=true' u'http://example.com?active=true&current=true' Personally, I would prefer to see the 'visible' representation of a url to NOT have ampersands quoted. Aside from problems with tracking urls, it breaks a usage pattern where cleaned text linkified : bleach.linkify(bleach.clean(input))) For example, we store 'cleaned' comments, but also linkify them... `linkify` This is still broken - perhaps it uses a different parser? Look carefully at the transformation of the querystring args on linkify active=true¤t=true active=true\xa4t=true *i did not get to dive into the tokenizer yet* but it superficially appears the parser sees the non-entity usage of `&` and is consuming the first 6 chars as if it were an entity (so `¤t=` becomes `\xa4t=true`). This is just a superficial guess as there are a few valid entities with 7 chars (`&1234567;`). In any event, with the above link an "expected" output of clean would be to not encode the ampersand in the attributes: u'http://example.com?active=true¤t=true' u'http://example.com?active=true&current=true'

While `Union` would be nice, it does not provide any public attributes to determine if a type is a `Union`.  The `__args__` contains the types as a tuple.  It is possible to determine if a type is a Union, but it's a bit hacky:

```python
>>> from typing import Union
>>> from doctor.types import string
>>> A = string('A', max_length=1)
>>> B = string('B', max_length=2)
>>> C = Union[A, B]
>>> C.__args__
(<class 'doctor.types.String'>, <class 'doctor.types.String'>)
>>> C.__origin__
typing.Union
>>> getattr(C, '__origin__', None) is Union
True
>>> getattr(A, '__origin__', None) is Union
False
```

Instead I propose this functionality simply get's built into a new type that can handle validation and that type can be subclassed in order to create your own type.  e.g.

```python
>>> from doctor.types import AnyOfType, string
>>> A = string('A', max_length=1)
>>> B = string('B', max_length=2)
>>> class C(AnyOfType):
...   any_of = [A, B]
...
>>> # the value 'aa' could only be of type B, since type A has max_length of 1
>>> C('aa')
'aa'
>>> # Type B can't be longer than 2 chars
>>> C('aaa')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/doctor/doctor/types.py", line 119, in __new__
    raise TypeSystemError(cls=cls, code='max_length')
doctor.errors.TypeSystemError: Must have no more than 2 characters.
```

Wanted to add one comment here: it seems to me like (once `--keep-oudated` is working per #966) it might be helpful to mention this strategy explicitly in documentation or in the "you may want to unpin your dependencies message".  That is to say, if you want to migrate from `requirements.txt` to `Pipfile`, leaving your dependencies unpinned but without upgrading them today, you want to:
- `pipenv install -r requirements.txt`
- change all your specifiers to `*` in `Pipfile`
- `pipenv lock --keep-outdated`
The last one is not at all obvious, and if you don't do it, you'll think you kept the pinned dependencies in `Pipfile.lock`, but actually as soon as you `pipenv install` they will get clobbered by the latest ones.

I realize this could be also useful between dev and test environment for modules.

In dev mode, the module must be installed in editable mode `-e .` to make development easy. 
However in test environment (CI test runner like Jenkins or GitlabCI), it would be better to install to install the package directly from the wheel, to be closer to how the package will be used in reality.

I know one case where this will definitely break - `multi-version installs`. If you use `easy_install --multi-version` to install your package, the package won't be visible on `__import__` without first being `required`, which is part of what `load_entry_point` does.

I also worry that all of this effort is for nothing since `easy_install` is largely deprecated in favor of installing with pip, which uses distlib, and [simply relying on distlib in easy_install scripts](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/pull/595) as well.

Considering the backward-incompatibility concerns here and the fact that it conflicts with the preferred approach, I'd rather suggest that we focus on advancing the work started in #595.

True

@sarthak5 appreciate your interest in dateparser. Thanks!

> Now integrating CLDR database can be done either by using yaml files for the data as currently used in dateparser or using json format as maintained in the CLDR repo. I think it should be better to use json files as it would lead to faster performance than using yaml. I am also thinking about adding support for calenders in CLDR database.

Without going too deep into the specifics, we like yaml for its rich feature set and its readability. Also, it has allowed us to retain language translations code in a very clean manner.

After having CLDR integration we expect dateparser to not only support all languages but also incorporate the locales bit of the information. For instance, standard date order for en-US is `mm-dd-yy` but for en-GB it is `dd-mm-yy`. This one will be an interesting one because as of now dateparser lacks the mechanism for defining locales. Also, some languages may only have one locale others multiple. Some may only differ in date order other also in parts of speech.

Plus, we also need to be mindful of how data from CLDR is going to be used with data we've with dateparser already which is very valuable and continues to be improved by the community.

P.S. You may want to attempt fixing this https://github.com/scrapinghub/dateparser/issues/253 it will help you get a neat idea of how dateparser defines and uses its language information.

Oh, astropy_helpers.egg-info is always created--that's a normal part of package initialization.  The .egg-info directory for a Python distribution (now also called .dist-info, though setuptools still calls it .egg-info by default for historical reasons) contains all the metadata about the package.  It is used, for example, by the `pkg_resources` module to discover information about installed distributions.  In the case of astropy_helpers, it gets created in the ah_bootstrap.py script so that the astropy_helpers package can be found via the submodule.  

`git clean` from the root of your repo wouldn't normally remove it, since it's in a submodule.  But you shouldn't have to delete it either.  I don't think the .egg-info dir has anything to do with your problem.

  - `guess_channels=False` new behaviour, where if it works with `numpy`, then it is probably the right thing?

  - `guess_channels=None` is always default but changes behaviour depending on the version number

  - 0.15: `None` behaves like `True` to retain the old behaviour. Raise a warning if `None` or `True` are set.

  - 0.17: `guess_channels` is an invalid parameter. Let python deal with the warnings/errors.

The problem is that you can't assume that 1em is enough for any icon. That's why `fa-fw` sets width to 1.28em (18/14em). If you override this setting, you will have issues. In other words: the issue is not related to `fa-fw` class.

You should use a different layout (http://jsfiddle.net/tagliala/UY9vQ/20/, refers to #1553 or take advantage of stack feature http://fontawesome.io/examples/#stacked or think about a slighter complex layout (add a container with `display: table` and add `display: table-cell` to icon)

Please also remeber that FA is not a silver bullet: icons have different width by design and some icons with odd height can't be vertically centered. Maybe that fontawesome is not the right solution for your specific use case.

It looks like that last error (returning 'foo' instead of the JSON) has to do with the rebuilding of the flask response object in decorator.py, and the fact that self.data is now a dict instead of a bytes object. 

```python
    def flask_response_object(self):
        """
        Builds an Flask response using the contained data,
        status_code, and headers.

        :rtype: flask.Response
        """
        self._response = flask.current_app.response_class(
            self.data, mimetype=self.mimetype, content_type=self.headers.get('content-type'),
            headers=self.headers)  # type: flask.Response
        self._response.status_code = self.status_code

        return self._response
```

One way to fix it would be to re-dump it here:
```python
 self._response = flask.current_app.response_class(
            json.dumps(self.data), mimetype=self.mimetype, content_type=self.headers.get('content-type'),
            headers=self.headers)  # type: flask.Response
```

Another solution could be to just return the original response if it was given

```python
    def flask_response_object(self):
        """
        Builds an Flask response using the contained data,
        status_code, and headers.

        :rtype: flask.Response
        """
        if not self._response:
            self._response = flask.current_app.response_class(
                self.data, mimetype=self.mimetype, content_type=self.headers.get('content-type'),
                headers=self.headers)  # type: flask.Response
            self._response.status_code = self.status_code

        return self._response
```

I wouldn't be surprised if these solutions introduced bugs or edge cases into the system, but I couldn't say for sure. If the general idea behind them looks alright, I would be happy to  solidify these, make some tests, and submit a pull request. If there would be a better way to solve this problem then just disregard me :)

I made a non-invasive work around for this with https://stedolan.github.io/jq/. This script assumes it is run in the same directory as the `.chalice` directory. 
```
#!/usr/bin/env bash

tier=$1
eval $(jq -r ".environment_variables | to_entries | map(\"export \(.key)=\(.value|tostring)\")|.[]" .chalice/config.json)
eval $(jq -r ".stages.$tier.environment_variables | to_entries | map(\"export \(.key)=\(.value|tostring)\")|.[]" .chalice/config.json)
exec chalice "${@:2}" --stage ${tier}
```

`pyreadline` was developed by the `jupyter` team, if I'm not mistaken.  They have a suitable replacement in `python_prompt` as they have their own console.  `dill` doesn't have a console, so until the above-referenced PR gets merged, or someone mods the code in that PR so it works to emulate `readline`… then there is no foreseeable alternate solution.  Using unmaintained code is typically not an issue until there is some community-wide change that it doesn't keep up with, especially when the dependency is only for a 2-liner as it is in `dill`.

So, if your only beef with `pyreadline` is that it's unmaintained, then it's not a pressing issue for `dill` at this time. In terms of speed, gluon seems to be still a little bit slower than the symbolic interface, but it seems to catch up (see upcoming 0.12 release of MXNet).
So the first step for us is to move from MXNet 0.10.0 to a newer MXNet version (probably 0.12), which we will do soon.

@monamu This is an issue with your template not SAM. SAM takes this dictionary you provide, 
```
Tags:
  prewarm: 
    Ref: AWS::NoValue
``` 
and produces the tag dictionary:
```
Tags:
  - Key: prewarm
    Value:
      Ref: AWS::NoValue
```

This will tell CloudFormation to omit the Value Key, which makes the template invalid and causes the error you are seeing.

I ran a quick test to verify this as well and seems to be accurate (below is vanilla CloudFormation to show the `Ref: AWS::NoValue` is invalid). Is this not what you are seeing as well?

This is a normal way to prove that you expand tests to capture a previous bug and then fix it after. If the bug wasn't captured by any tests before, then no one can see how they fail.

In this case, we change the way that the Tox works in order to capture a failure in the existing code base.. Initially, I thought we could prove the failure by removing `usedevelop` from `tox.ini` and instead installing the built .whl in the tox env.

If you are implementing some addition or change and adding new test coverage or changing existing tests, the test failure isn't interesting. With a single TDD commit, you can prove that tests succeed and that they cover the codebase... but sometimes the more interesting part is to prove that they fail :)

e.g. you'd remove all files that are not on disk anymore like his:
`housekeeper delete files --tag fastq --unassociated`

Remove all bam files before a certain date:
`housekeeper delete files --tag bam --before 2017-06-15`

Remove fastq files from a flowcell:
`housekeeper delete files --tag fastq --tag H0HKKALXX`

It'll always ask for confirmation, unless you add `-y`:
`housekeeper delete files --bundle sillyfish -y`

If you do not provide a `--tag` or `--bundle`, essentially deleting everything, the function will not let you do that.

There usually is someone who sees that a new tool/library/package/etc. is a good idea, and now has to either fully "sell" it to other persons with the information from the new tool's feature sheet (then leading to an official evaluation maybe), or start small and show the benefits in an existing project. We have an issue to track this here: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/252

No comments on it, which I assume means not much demand.  Unfortunately, there's no voting functionality for github issues, so users have to post something to let us know they care about an issue/feature.

The long answer:

Right now we're focusing our efforts on getting a python code generator solution for the various management apis.  When that's ready this will greatly reduce the amount of code we have to manually write/maintain (for management apis).

The code generator may then evolve to be able to handle the storage runtime apis.  That would be great, as long as the storage team produces metadata for it.  If that happens, we'll probably refresh the whole storage implementation based on that, with additional manually implemented layer on top for ease of use (where it matters).

The difficult part is that for digest auth there is potentially a second request that needs to be launched, and the whole point of the helper is so that you don't have to worry about handling that additional request. If you want to be able to do use the `client.get(url, auth=aiohttp.DigestAuth...)` syntax, the easiest way to go about it would be to wrap the `aiohttp.ClientSession._request` method. This way, the digest auth can make multiple calls to the underlying `_request` method.

This is kind of what I had been asking about earlier though. @alxpy had mentioned doing `aiohttp.ClientSession(digest_auth=...)` but I thought it sounded like you guys didn't like that. I think for the users `client.get(url, auth=aiohttp.DigestAuth...)` is the best option but I think it will be a little messier to implement. At the end of the day though, I'm fine with however you guys want to do it.

Umm... hmm. Ok. It seems like you have some very severe issues going on with your two installations of python conflicting. It is generally a good idea to only use one way of installing python otherwise you can end up in situations like this 😞 

I think you need to make a decision about whether you want to use anaconda or not, and then stick with that for everything. For example, if you decide you want to use anaconda, then uninstall nbgrader, jupyterhub, and jupyter from your system python, and then reinstall them with anaconda python:

```
/usr/local/bin/pip uninstall nbgrader jupyter jupyterhub
/usr/local/anaconda3/bin/pip install -U nbgrader jupyter jupyterhub
/usr/local/anaconda3/bin/jupyter nbextension install --overwrite --sys-prefix --py nbgrader
/usr/local/anaconda3/bin/jupyter nbextension enable --sys-prefix --py nbgrader
/usr/local/anaconda3/bin/jupyter serverextension enable --sys-prefix --py nbgrader
```

Then, make sure the `/usr/local/anaconda3/bin` is the first thing in your `$PATH`.

**Alternately**, if you want to use your system python, then uninstall nbgrader, jupyter, and jupyterhub from your anaconda installation and reinstall them with system python:

```
/usr/local/anaconda3/bin/pip uninstall nbgrader jupyter jupyterhub
/usr/local/bin/pip install -U nbgrader jupyter jupyterhub
/usr/local/bin/jupyter nbextension install --overwrite --sys-prefix --py nbgrader
/usr/local/bin/jupyter nbextension enable --sys-prefix --py nbgrader
/usr/local/bin/jupyter serverextension enable --sys-prefix --py nbgrader
```

Then, make sure anaconda isn't anywhere on your `$PATH`.

That is my fundamental objection with the design here.  The strong versioning goes a long way, but not all the way, toward ameliorating some of the problems with that.  The necessities/advantages of installing javascript content through npm seems to only apply to primarily client side extensions, and cut against the needs of kernel-dependent extensions, where atomic installation is important.

All that said, I'll apologize again for coming late to this discussion.  Certainly from matplotlib's perspective, this seems like a regression for both users (which have an additional manual installation step and the possibility for confusion and more toes to shoot oneself in) and developers (that must release packages on two different package frameworks, where before there was one), with little benefit *in our particular use case*.  But obviously, we are but one case and will go with the flow if the benefits for other kinds of extensions outweigh the disadvantages for our kind.

I am a huge fan of linearized histories.  (I'm honestly not sure how we ended up with that merge mishap.)

Some of my thoughts:
 - We should adopt a process for adding contributions that ensures the history remains linear.
 - We should keep merge commits.
   - Topic branches provide a hierarchical overlay structure that is worth preserving.
   - PRs then work as a sort of higher-level "commit"
   - Very useful with `git-bisect`, for example.
 - However, merge commits should also be functionally superfluous
   - every topic branch should be fast-forward-able.
   - requires every "merge" to have a strict ordering.
     - merges need to be sequential and therefore coordinated.
     - :-1: for @thewtex
 - I argue:
   - Maintaining a clean, easy-to-follow, linear history is worth the extra work.
   - For projects at our current size, it's not that much "extra" work.
   - If we grow, we should be able to scale our process by introducing sub-topics.
     - For example, a `testing` subtopic where @msmolens and I could be working on our own, integrating #100, #98, and #46.  In this case, we only need to coordinate merges amongst ourselves, and can integrate to master when ready.

Thank you @ciscomonkey !  I did catch this with the new test suite I am working to complete, but I haven't pushed the commits to master yet.  I will get this done soon and publish a patched release.

My only concern / challenge with using a different "parameter" to enable this functionality is that it would cause the function to return some different "types" of objects (which might be confusing to some):

1. No Parameter - A `GeneratorContainer` that works as described.
2. With `items=` parameter, there could be a couple of options:
    * A `GeneratorContainer` that yields at max the number of `items=` requested when the container was created.  Since, the `GeneratorContainers` can be used over and over again, I can see situations where users might run into issues with it only **ever** returning just so many items.  Plus if you are really only going to request a fixed number of items... You probably want to save those in a list or etc. so that you can simply re-use the local list of items, which leads to the other option.
    * A list with the specified number of items, but now the method would be returning either a `GeneratorContainer` or a `list`, which could definitely be confusing.

That's why I am partial to adding this via a convenience slicing feature.  It would work like an optimized version of the `islice()` solution above, and would hopefully be intuitive:

"The `list()` methods return `GeneratorContainers`, which can be sliced to yield or limit the number of items returned."

I think @RonnyPfannschmidt means something like this:

1. Mark your slow/integration tests with a mark, say `slow`.
2. Run your **regular** tests using xdist: `pytest -n auto -m "not slow"`
3. Run your **slow** tests without xdist: `pytest -m slow`.

It should be simple to change your CI system to run pytest twice instead of only once. You can have even more layers of test sessions if you prefer.

You are probably looking for stats endpoint, the below will get you a raw stats output:

For example:
```
https://yourltm/mgmt/tm/ltm/node/~Common~192.168.15.15/stats
```
This can be achieved in SDK with:

````
from f5.bigip import ManagementRoot
mr = ManagementRoot('yourltm', 'user', 'pass')
node = mr.tm.ltm.nodes.node.load(name='192.168.15.15', partition='Common')
stats = node.stats.load()

The above will give you raw stats in a json blob.

````
If you want to access those using dot notation you can use the utility I have built, please bear in mind as it is a very early release of that so some endpoints might not work, the more people use it and report issues etc, the better i can make it:

https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/blob/development/f5/utils/responses/test/functional/test_handlers.py

In the SDK this will work like so:
````
from f5.utils.responses.handlers import Stats
from f5.bigip import ManagementRoot

mr = ManagementRoot('yourltm', 'user', 'pass')
node = mr.tm.ltm.nodes.node.load(name='192.168.15.15', partition='Common')

node_stat = Stats(node.stats.load())

>>> node_stat.stat.curSessions.value
Out[10]: 2

>>> node_stat.stat.serverside_curConns.value
Out[10]: 10

````
In case i misunderstood you and you want connection table output we do not have this endpoint in the SDK yet, in REST it is accessible under:

``
https://yourltm/mgmt/tm/sys/performance/connections

``
Probably you can work around this by using TMSH over REST and issuing TMSH commands but it depends if this is what you want.

Hi,
While the behaviour is admittedly quite disconcerting, I'm not quite sure this is a valid bug since RFC5545 says:
```
Recurrence rules may generate recurrence instances with an invalid
date (e.g., February 30) or nonexistent local time (e.g., 1:30 AM
on a day where the local time is moved forward by an hour at 1:00
AM).  Such recurrence instances MUST be ignored and MUST NOT be
counted as part of the recurrence set.

It's not really worth fixing or even documenting though, because the whole idea of logic names is flawed and the SMT community is currently working out a solution. Ideally, Z3 should work without specifying a logic, as it will figure out what it needs and where it can cut corners (but that doesn't always work either...).

I've been running into this with the R kernel too. I _think_ this stems from the R kernel not booting as fast as we expect and so it misses our `kernel_info_request`. In general we need to retry our initial `kernel_info_request` so that we can serve kernels that are slower to start more effectively. In order to have Python kernels be detected, they need to be installed in a similar manner as the R kernel:

```
python -m pip install ipykernel
python -m ipykernel install --user
```

This installs a kernelspec for Python. You can also run `ipykernel` with options, including the name you want to make the kernel (in case you want individual ones for conda environments).

In the long run once https://github.com/nteract/nteract/pull/1264 is finished up, there are small PRs ahead to detect and use the default Python kernel as well as conda environments.

The state explosion here is not polynomial here - it is exponential. Each time a path goes through the inner loop, it splits in two. After the first loop, there are two paths, after the second, four, after the third, 8, etc.

For BUFF_LEN = 5, there are 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10 loops, so there are 2^10 = 1024 paths in the end.

For BUFF_LEN = 6, there are 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 15 loops, so there are 2^15 = 32768 paths.

I don't know what the "good engineering" solution to this is, but my answer is gonna be that you need to write your own strcpy simprocedure that emulates only the functionality that you actually need to solve this problem. I feel like 0x1000 is way more than angr will realistically be able to handle.

If you need to use the LHCb version of Python 2.7 to run the tests then the following will install an appropriate version of `py.test`:
```bash
$ SetupProject Ganga
$ wget https://bootstrap.pypa.io/get-pip.py
$ python2.7 get-pip.py --user
$ pip install --user pytest  # only need run this once
```
you can then run the tests using it with:
```bash
$ cd ganga  # This is the local git checkout of ganga
$ SetupProject Ganga
$ PYTHONPATH=python:$PYTHONPATH ~/.local/bin/py.test python/GangaLHCb/test/GPI/
```

In general, I agree that such naming conventions should be uses for libraries.  

Be aware it could be misleading in this instance since librealsense has a built in check to ensure that the the binary linked to the libraries was compiled with the **exact** version, and fails to create a context if it does not match for older releases.

There have been recent improvements to allow librealsense with different patch version to be considered compatible (1.11.**X**), but not minor version. 
So when librealsense.so.1.**12**.0 is released, you can't update to the new library and still run binaries which were originally liked to librealsense1.11.1 -- it will fail.

If these shared object naming conventions are going to be implemented, it would be nice to have minor and patch versions both remain ABI compatible -- remain backward compatible.  
If changes need to be made that break the ABI, then bump the major version.

See:
https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/cf6f1ca4b7687b04007c0c8a17ef509200af909a/src/rs.cpp#L75

Hello,

First of all I would advise that you use either the command-line interface or "dicom2nifti.convert_directory" unless you are 100% certain that there is only one series in the directory.

About the error. This occurs when trying to convert compressed dicom files (jpeg, jpeg2000, ...) which are not natively supported. For this we use the gdcmconv command line tool. There are 2 ways to make it work. 
Install gdcmconv from libgdcm-tools (http://gdcm.sourceforge.net/wiki/index.php/Main_Page) and make certain it is globally from your path. 
Or install it and set the path to gdcmconv using "dicom2nifti.settings.set_gdcmconv_path(path)".

We will try to document these features a little better with a future release.

Thanks for submitting this PR.  This looks good overall.  Most my comments are minor nits.  Two things I wanted to up-level:

1. When sending MLE Advertisements, Thread only specifies use of Trickle when operating as a Router.  A REED uses a fixed interval with random jitter.
2. So far, OpenThread's implementation philosophy is to implement the necessary functionality required by Thread to minimize resources.  This PR pushes that boundary by being fully RFC 6206 compliant, in particular suppression which is not used by Thread.

One reason to allow line params in cell is that existing magics get it for free. For example, in
https://github.com/googledatalab/notebooks/blob/master/tutorials/BigQuery/BigQuery%20Commands.ipynb, we have:

%bq sample --table cloud-datalab-samples.httplogs.logs_20140615 --count 10 --fields timestamp,latency --method hashed --key-field latency

And now it can be:

%bq sample
table: cloud-datalab-samples.httplogs.logs_20140615
count: 10
fields: timestamp,latency
method: hashed
key-field: latency

If we explicitly force the separation of line and cell params, we will have to touch all the magics to move line params to cell, and it will be a breaking change.

Oh that's very interesting. Hm, are you suggesting basically making the invocations of `run_incrementer` dependent on the actors `__init__` task?

When an actor handle `A` is passed to a task `T`, there seem to be a couple of options:
1. Don't introduce any dependencies (current behavior).
2. Make `T` depend on the actor creation event.
3. Make `T` depend on the actor's `__init__` task.
4. Make `T` depend on the most recent task issued using the handle `A`.

Options 2, 3, and 4 all solve the above problem.

I think either 1, 2, or 4 make sense (maybe 2 makes the most sense). However 2 will not be possible until we start treating actor creation as a task.

We already have 4 in some sense because the first method invoked on the actor from within `T` will depend on the last task issued using `A`.

This could be a use case for "execution edges" outside of actor methods, right?

In batch files in windows argument looks different than in CMD window.

You use double quotes if there are empty spaces, but sometimes this is not enough.

This is because some special characters have to be typed 2 times in batch file, 
and then you see only one character in CMD window.

For example, empty spaces are not allowed in FFmpeg arguments, and where is empty space in 
batch file you replace it with %%20, in CMD window you see %20.

You know that you got it right if you see command "as is" in CMD window.

@smurfix I have to admit that looking at https://github.com/python-trio/trio/compare/sigchld in its current state is mostly just reminding me why I don't want to support SIGCHLD. That's a *ton* of code and complexities exposed in the public API.

As far as `await` being a special kind of function call goes, yes, that's exactly what it is - it's a function call that also serves as a local suspend point for concurrency management purposes.

The equivalent to forgetting an await in synchronous code is to call a factory function (thus getting a callable back), and then forgetting to call the returned callable that actually does what you want.

The difference is that in the latter case you don't get any warning at all (see previous comment about there being no reasonable opportunity to complain about "uncalled functions"), while in the asynchronous case we can be more confident that if someone got as far as creating the coroutine (rather than just referencing the coroutine factory), they actually meant to wait for it run at some point (similar to the way interpreter shutdown auto-joins non-daemon threads).

Excellent question! I've been meaning to do something like this, but have been slow to respond because I'm cranky about the available options :-).

* IRC is obviously a thing, but it's extremely unfriendly to those who haven't already been initiated into its arcane secrets. Obviously I have a bouncer set up etc., but I hesitate to demand that of users. Though I guess we could direct them to [IRCCloud](https://www.irccloud.com/). It really seems like we could ought to be able to do better in 2017.

* Gitter is a thing, it works for lots of projects, but I'm not very happy with it – the UI tends to bog down in my browser, it's a walled-garden proprietary model, and it's always sending me notifications hours late or not at all. I'm not *that* worried about lock-in on a chat room, switching is not that hard, but again it seems like we ought to be able to do better. Also, I seem to have somehow gotten into a state where there's a [python-trio organization](https://gitter.im/python-trio) (with a distorted logo, for some reason), but it contains zero rooms and I can't add any.

* Slack is popular but extremely unfriendly for this purpose, because of how difficult it makes it for someone new to jump in and start talking. Also super proprietary.

* [Zulip](https://zulip.org/) is a neat open-source chat program, there's a company behind it that I'm told is willing to host it for us for free, and it's possible to do login-via-github, though they don't yet have any kind of anonymous access. The downside here is that it's really designed more for team collaboration; the UI is somewhat confusing and overwhelming when you first log in. Every conversation is labeled with a room + a tag, so conversations thread, but each thread is broken up over days, and when you first log in you don't get "chat log + blinking cursor to add something", you get a bunch of different colored boxes with conversation fragments and have to click on one to reply or else click "start new topic". If anyone's curious you can log into their project chat server [here](https://chat.zulip.org/accounts/login/) and look around.

* There are some other open-source chat apps that exist: Matrix and its Riot client. Mattermost. Probably others. I don't know too much about these and don't see an obvious way to set them up. I'd rather not have to host something myself.

* [Discourse](https://www.discourse.org/) is generally in this area, not quite the "quick chat" thing but it overlaps. Unfortunately the hosted version is $100/mo which is *super* expensive for a hobby project. (And they mighhht do [free open source hosting](https://blog.discourse.org/2016/03/free-discourse-forum-hosting-for-community-friendly-github-projects/) but I don't get the sense that they're interested in talking to small projects.) I could host it myself somewhere, but I'm pretty sure eventually something would break and it would take me a few weeks to fix and ugh.

* We could do a mailman mailing list. Python.org would be happy to host it.

Is it really 2017 and IRC and mailman are the only decent free options? Anyone else want to weigh in? Maybe I need to try and figure out who to contact to get gitter to unwedge itself...

In terms of pseudo-code, I presume it might look something like:

```python
def styleText(self, start, end):
        self.startStyling(start)
        text = self.parent().text()[start:end]

        for token in self.lark_parser.lex(text):
            self.setStyling( len(bytearray(token, "utf-8")), self.style.get(token.type, 0))
```
Where `self.style` is a dictionary of `token_name -> style-number`.

Interesting, the Antlr4 [syntax](https://github.com/antlr/grammars-v4) is also quite clean, example:

    variable_identifier : 
        : IDENTIFIER
        ;
    primary_expression : 
        : variable_identifier
        | INTCONSTANT
        | UINTCONSTANT
        | FLOATCONSTANT
        | BOOLCONSTANT
        | DOUBLECONSTANT
        | LEFT_PAREN expression RIGHT_PAREN
        ;
    postfix_expression : 
        : primary_expression
        | postfix_expression LEFT_BRACKET integer_expression RIGHT_BRACKET
        | function_call
        | postfix_expression DOT FIELD_SELECTION
        | postfix_expression INC_OP
        | postfix_expression DEC_OP
        ;
